metadata.title: SaaS Security Customer Framework (SSCF) v1.0 Control Catalog
metadata.remarks: SSCF v1.0 full catalog: 36 controls across 6 domains (CON, DSP, IAM, IPY, LOG, SEF). Each control carries a ccm-controls prop referencing the authoritative CSA CCM v4.1 control IDs. This catalog is the base for platform-specific OSCAL profiles (SBS for Salesforce, WSCC for Workday). CCM v4.1 regulatory crosswalk (SOX, HIPAA, SOC2, ISO 27001, NIST 800-53 r5, PCI DSS v4, GDPR) applies to all referenced CCM controls.

links.text: CSA Cloud Controls Matrix v4.1 (authoritative CCM source)

props.name: domain-description
props.value: Controls ensuring SaaS platforms are deployed and maintained against approved security baselines, with drift detection and credential lifecycle governance.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Apply and continuously assess approved secure configuration baselines for each SaaS platform against a published benchmark.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Each SaaS platform must have a documented security baseline approved by the Security Team. Baseline controls must be assessed at least quarterly via automated tooling. Deviations must be tracked as findings with assigned owners and remediation deadlines. The baseline must be updated when the vendor releases new security features or configuration options relevant to the organization's risk profile.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that an approved baseline exists for the platform, automated assessment occurs at least quarterly, all current deviations are tracked with owners and deadlines, and the baseline is current with vendor guidance.

controls.title: Drift Detection and Baseline Deviation Management

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Detect unauthorized configuration drift from the approved security baseline and enforce a remediation workflow with defined SLAs.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Drift detection must be continuous or near-real-time for critical configuration items. Detection of baseline deviation must trigger an automated alert routed to the platform owner and Security Team. Remediation SLAs: Critical drift — 24 hours; High drift — 7 days; Moderate drift — 30 days. Unresolved drift beyond SLA must escalate to management review.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that drift detection is active for critical baseline items, alerts route to owners and security, and remediation SLAs are defined and tracked.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Govern the full lifecycle of API credentials, OAuth tokens, integration system passwords, and service account secrets used to access SaaS platforms.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: All API credentials must be inventoried in a secrets manager. Rotation intervals: integration passwords — 90 days max; OAuth refresh tokens — 1 year max; API keys — 180 days max. Credentials must not be stored in code repositories, config files, or incident tickets. Revocation must be immediate on personnel departure or suspected compromise.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that all API credentials are inventoried, rotation schedules are enforced, no long-lived credentials exceed policy limits, and revocation procedures are documented and tested.

controls.title: Platform Hardening and Security Settings Verification

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Verify that vendor-published hardening guidance and security settings are applied to each SaaS platform, including disabling insecure defaults and enabling platform-native security features.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Hardening must address: disabling legacy authentication protocols, enabling encryption-at-rest and in-transit for all tenant data, configuring platform-native DLP rules, disabling unused features and integrations, and applying IP allowlisting where the platform supports it. Hardening settings must be documented in a platform runbook and reviewed after each major platform update.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that vendor hardening guidance has been applied, insecure defaults are disabled, and platform-native security features are enabled and configured per policy.

controls.title: Third-Party Integration and Supply Chain Security

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Inventory and assess the security posture of all third-party integrations and OAuth-connected applications accessing the SaaS platform.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: All third-party integrations must be inventoried with documented business justification, data access scope, and security assessment status. OAuth-connected apps must be reviewed quarterly; apps without documented approval must be revoked. High-risk integrations (those with write access to sensitive data) require annual security review. Integrations that have not been used in 90 days must be revoked.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that a current integration inventory exists, all integrations have documented approvals and scoped permissions, unauthorized or unused integrations are revoked, and high-risk integrations have current security assessments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Track and assure the application of security-relevant vendor patches, updates, and deprecation of end-of-life SaaS platform versions or features.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Application owners must monitor vendor security bulletins and release notes. Critical security patches must be applied within 30 days of release; high-severity within 90 days. End-of-life or deprecated features must be disabled within 60 days of vendor EOL notice. Patch application status must be documented in the platform runbook and reported to Security Team quarterly.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that vendor security bulletins are monitored, critical patches are applied within SLA, no EOL features are in active use, and patch status is documented and reported.

props.name: domain-description
props.value: Controls protecting sensitive business and personal data stored in, processed by, or transiting through SaaS platforms.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Restrict and continuously monitor access to sensitive business and personal data fields within SaaS platforms, aligned to data classification policy.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Sensitive data categories include: PII (SSN, DOB, government ID), financial data (compensation, account numbers), health data (PHI), and credentials. Access must be scoped to business need with documented justification. Field-level masking or encryption is required where the platform supports it. Access reviews must occur quarterly for roles with sensitive data permissions.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that sensitive data fields are identified in the platform, access is restricted to documented roles, field-level controls are enabled where available, and access is reviewed quarterly.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Prevent unauthorized bulk data exports, report downloads, and API-based exfiltration from SaaS platforms.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Controls must include: export volume thresholds with automated alerts, restriction of export permissions to documented roles, prohibition of exports to personal storage (Dropbox, personal email), and logging of all bulk export events. External sharing links must have expiry dates and require authentication.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that export permissions are restricted, volume-based alerts are active, exports are logged with user attribution, and external sharing controls prevent unauthenticated or indefinite access.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Maintain a current data inventory and classification map for all data stored or processed within each SaaS platform, aligned to the organization's data classification policy.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Data classification must identify: data types present (PII, financial, health, proprietary), applicable regulations per data type, data owner and steward, and sensitivity tier (public, internal, confidential, restricted). Classification must be updated when new data flows are introduced or platform features change the data processing scope. Data stewards are responsible for maintaining accuracy.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that a current data inventory exists for the platform, all data types are classified per policy, ownership is assigned, and the inventory is reviewed at least annually.

controls.title: Cross-Border Data Transfer and Residency Controls

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Ensure that cross-border data transfers comply with applicable data sovereignty requirements, and that SaaS platform data residency settings align with organizational policy and regulatory obligations.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: For regulated data (GDPR personal data, HIPAA PHI, data subject to export controls): data residency must be configured to the approved jurisdiction, cross-border transfer mechanisms (SCCs, BCRs, adequacy decisions) must be documented and current. SaaS vendor's data processing agreements must specify processing locations. Annual review required when vendor infrastructure changes.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that data residency is configured per policy, cross-border transfer legal mechanisms are in place and current, and vendor DPAs accurately reflect processing locations.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Apply and enforce data retention schedules and secure deletion procedures for data stored in SaaS platforms, ensuring data is not retained beyond its authorized lifecycle.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Retention schedules must be defined per data classification and regulation (e.g., HIPAA: 6 years, SOX: 7 years, GDPR: storage limitation principle). SaaS platforms must support or integrate with automated deletion workflows. At retention expiry, data must be cryptographically erased or documented as destroyed. Retention holds for legal proceedings must override scheduled deletion and be tracked.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that retention schedules are defined, automated deletion is configured or documented, secure deletion is cryptographic, and retention holds are tracked separately.

controls.title: Privacy Rights and Data Subject Access Management

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Ensure SaaS platform data handling supports organizational obligations to fulfill data subject rights requests (access, correction, deletion, portability) within regulatory timeframes.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Platform data flows must be mapped to support DSAR fulfillment. For GDPR-regulated data: access, rectification, erasure, and portability requests must be fulfillable within 30 days. For CCPA: deletion and opt-out requests within 45 days. Platforms must support data export in machine-readable format for portability requests. Privacy team must be documented as the responsible owner for DSAR coordination.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that DSAR fulfillment procedures are documented, data flows support data subject access and deletion, and response time SLAs meet regulatory requirements.

props.name: domain-description
props.value: Controls governing who can access SaaS platforms, under what conditions, and with what level of privilege — from workforce federation through service account lifecycle.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Require multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all privileged accounts and all workforce accounts accessing SaaS platforms.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: MFA must be enforced at the IdP or platform level, not merely offered as optional. Exemptions require documented risk acceptance with compensating controls and time-bound review. FIDO2/WebAuthn or authenticator-app OTP are preferred; SMS OTP is discouraged for privileged roles.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that MFA is enforced (not optional) for all interactive user sessions accessing the assessed SaaS platform, and that no active user accounts have MFA disabled without documented exception.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Enforce formal approval, least-privilege assignment, and periodic recertification (at minimum annually) for all privileged roles in SaaS platforms.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Privileged roles include any role granting administrative access, data export capability, security configuration changes, or access to sensitive personal or financial data. Recertification must be performed by a role owner who is not the account holder. Orphaned privileged accounts must be disabled within 30 days of the role owner's departure.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that all privileged role assignments have documented approvals, are scoped to least privilege, and have been recertified within the past 12 months.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Enforce enterprise IdP federation and harden SSO policy posture. Local authentication bypass paths must be disabled or restricted to break-glass accounts only.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: SSO must route through the enterprise IdP (e.g., Okta, Azure AD, Ping). Local password login must be disabled for normal user accounts. Break-glass accounts require documented inventory, individual passwords stored in PAM, and quarterly review. SAML assertions must be signed; SCIM provisioning is strongly preferred over manual provisioning.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that SSO is enforced via enterprise IdP, that local login is disabled or restricted to break-glass accounts with PAM-managed credentials, and that break-glass accounts are inventoried and reviewed quarterly.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Ensure timely and complete revocation of SaaS platform access upon employee termination, role change, or contract end — and that regular access recertification identifies and removes stale accounts.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Offboarding: all SaaS access must be revoked within 24 hours of termination (HR trigger to IdP/SCIM deprovisioning is preferred). For role changes, access not required in the new role must be revoked within 7 days. Quarterly access reviews must identify accounts that have not logged in within 90 days for review/removal. Shared or team accounts are prohibited.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that offboarding deprovisioning is automated or completed within 24 hours, that role-change access reviews occur, and that stale account detection and removal is operational.

controls.title: Service Account and Non-Human Identity Governance

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Maintain a complete inventory of all non-human identities (service accounts, integration users, automation identities) accessing SaaS platforms, with documented owners and scoped permissions.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Every service account must have a named human owner, a documented business purpose, scoped permissions (least privilege), and a defined rotation schedule for associated credentials. Service accounts must not have interactive login capability unless documented with compensating controls. Accounts without an active owner must be disabled within 30 days. Annual review of all service accounts is mandatory.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that a service account inventory exists, all accounts have named owners and documented purposes, permissions are least-privilege, and annual review has occurred within the past 12 months.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Configure SaaS platform session controls to enforce idle timeout, limit concurrent sessions, and restrict session tokens to authorized devices or networks.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Session idle timeout must be configured to no more than 8 hours for standard users and 30 minutes for privileged users. Session tokens must be invalidated on logout (not just cleared client-side). Token lifetime must align with MFA re-authentication requirements. Concurrent session limits must be enforced where the platform supports it. Sessions from unmanaged devices or unapproved geolocations must require step-up authentication.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that idle timeout is configured per policy, session tokens are server-side invalidated on logout, and step-up authentication is triggered for high-risk session attributes.

controls.title: Just-in-Time Access and Temporary Privilege Elevation

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Govern temporary privilege elevation through a formal request-approve-expire workflow, avoiding standing privileged access for operational tasks.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: JIT access controls must require: a business justification, approval from a role owner or security delegate, a maximum session duration (4 hours for most tasks, 24 hours maximum), and automatic revocation at expiry without manual intervention. All JIT sessions must generate audit log entries. PAM tooling is preferred for SaaS platforms that support SCIM or API-based role assignment.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that JIT workflows are in place, temporary privileges expire automatically, all JIT sessions are logged, and standing privileged access is minimized with documented exceptions.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Govern access granted to external parties (contractors, partners, auditors) including scoped permissions, time-bound access, MFA requirements, and regular recertification.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: External users must be provisioned via a separate identity pool or clearly labeled in the directory. Access must be scoped to the minimum data and function required. MFA is mandatory for all external accounts. Access must have an explicit expiry date (maximum 12 months, renewable with re-approval). Quarterly review must verify continued business need. External access must never include administrative or privileged roles without CISO-level approval.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that all external accounts are identifiable, time-bound, MFA-enforced, and have been recertified within the past quarter.

props.name: domain-description
props.value: Controls ensuring SaaS platforms support data portability, open API standards, integration security, and continuity of access without vendor lock-in risk.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Verify that SaaS platforms support data export in open, machine-readable formats sufficient to enable migration, regulatory fulfillment, and business continuity without proprietary lock-in.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Platforms must provide: bulk data export in standard formats (CSV, JSON, XML, or OSCAL where applicable), documented export API endpoints with rate limits, export of complete audit logs in a portable format, and a vendor-exit data migration procedure. Export capabilities must be tested at least annually as part of business continuity planning. Vendor contracts must specify data return SLAs on termination.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that standard-format export is available and tested, vendor-exit data return SLAs are contractually defined, and export procedures are documented in the platform runbook.

controls.title: API Security and Open Interface Governance

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Govern all API access to SaaS platforms to ensure that APIs enforce authentication, authorization, rate limiting, and input validation consistent with OWASP API Security standards.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: API access must use short-lived tokens (OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials or Bearer tokens). Unauthenticated API endpoints must not expose sensitive data. Rate limiting must be configured to prevent enumeration and DoS. API usage must be logged with sufficient detail to support forensic investigation. Deprecated or legacy API versions must be disabled within 90 days of successor release.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that all API access is authenticated via short-lived tokens, rate limiting is configured, API calls are logged, and deprecated API versions are disabled.

controls.title: Integration Inventory and Third-Party Connector Risk Assessment

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Maintain an inventory of all integrations connecting to SaaS platforms and assess the security risk of data flows between connected systems.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: The integration inventory must capture: connected system name and owner, data exchanged (types and sensitivity), authentication method, data flow direction, and security assessment status. Integrations transmitting sensitive data must use encrypted channels (TLS 1.2+). Annual vendor security assessments (SOC 2, ISO 27001, or equivalent) must be obtained for high-risk integrations. Webhook endpoints must require signature verification.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that an integration inventory exists and is current, sensitive data flows use encrypted channels, and high-risk integrations have current vendor security certifications.

controls.title: Vendor Lock-In Mitigation and SaaS Exit Planning

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Document a SaaS platform exit plan that includes data retrieval procedures, migration timelines, and dependency mapping to enable continuity of business operations in the event of vendor exit.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Exit planning must address: all data stored in the platform (including metadata and audit history), downstream systems dependent on the platform's data or APIs, contractual data-return obligations and timelines, and an estimated migration effort. Exit plans must be reviewed annually or upon significant platform contract renewal. Critical-path platforms must have an alternate vendor or interim solution identified.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that a documented exit plan exists for the platform, it addresses all stored data and downstream dependencies, and the plan has been reviewed within the past 12 months.

controls.title: Multi-Cloud Data Residency and Sovereignty Controls

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Ensure that SaaS platform configurations enforce data residency requirements and that data synchronized across cloud environments or geographies complies with applicable sovereignty regulations.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Where regulated data is present: platform must be configured to restrict processing to approved jurisdictions. Vendor documentation must confirm sub-processor locations. Any multi-cloud synchronization (backup, disaster recovery replication) must remain within the approved data residency zone. Annual verification of vendor sub-processor list against approved jurisdictions is required.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that data residency is enforced at the platform configuration level, sub-processor locations are documented and within approved jurisdictions, and annual verification has occurred.

props.name: domain-description
props.value: Controls ensuring SaaS platform activities are logged sufficiently to support detection, investigation, compliance, and audit — forwarded to centralized monitoring infrastructure.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Enable all required audit and security logs in SaaS platforms sufficient to support detection, forensics, and compliance reporting.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Required log types include: authentication events (success and failure), privileged action audit, data export/download, configuration changes, and API access. Logs must be forwarded to a centralized SIEM within 24 hours of generation. Native logging must not be the only copy.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that all required log categories are enabled in the platform, that logs are forwarded to a centralized SIEM, and that at least 90 days of logs are accessible for investigation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Capture and monitor all administrative actions and security configuration changes within SaaS platforms.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Admin audit logs must capture: who made the change, what was changed, the before and after state where supported, and timestamp in UTC. Alerts must be configured for high-risk admin actions (e.g., disabling MFA, changing security policies, granting privileged roles). Log tampering controls must be in place.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that admin action logging is enabled, that alerts fire on high-risk config changes, and that audit logs are stored in a tamper-evident system.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Retain audit logs per organizational policy (minimum 12 months hot, 36 months cold) and preserve log integrity to support investigations and regulatory requests.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Retention requirements may vary by data classification and regulation (HIPAA: 6 years; SOX: 7 years; SOC2: per audit cycle). Hash-based or write-once log storage is required for integrity. Log deletion must require dual approval. Restoration from cold storage must be tested annually.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that retention policies meet the highest applicable regulatory requirement, that integrity controls prevent unauthorized deletion or modification, and that restoration has been tested.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Configure real-time monitoring rules and alerts for high-risk events in SaaS platforms, routed to the Security Operations team within defined SLA thresholds.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Mandatory alert conditions include: failed authentication exceeding threshold, privilege escalation events, bulk data access or export, configuration changes to security settings, and access from anomalous geolocations or impossible travel. Alert routing must reach the SOC queue within 5 minutes of event detection. Alert suppression rules must be documented and reviewed quarterly.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that mandatory alert conditions are configured, alert routing delivers to SOC within 5 minutes, and suppression rules are documented and reviewed.

controls.title: SIEM Integration and Cross-Platform Log Correlation

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Integrate SaaS platform logs into the centralized SIEM to enable cross-platform correlation, incident investigation, and compliance reporting.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: SIEM integration must deliver all required log types (see SSCF-LOG-001) in a normalized format (CEF, LEEF, or native SIEM connector). SaaS log sources must be documented in the SIEM asset inventory. Correlation rules linking SaaS events to identity data (IdP logs) and endpoint telemetry must exist for high-risk scenarios. Integration health monitoring must alert on log source outages within 1 hour.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that the SaaS platform is integrated with the SIEM, logs are normalized and searchable, cross-platform correlation rules exist, and integration health monitoring is active.

controls.title: Anomalous Behavior Detection and Insider Threat Monitoring

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Apply behavioral analytics or UEBA capabilities to detect anomalous user activity patterns within SaaS platforms that may indicate insider threat, account compromise, or data misuse.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Behavioral baselines must be established for: normal data access volumes, working hours patterns, and typical API usage. Deviations exceeding defined thresholds (e.g., data access 10x above baseline, access outside business hours from new IP) must generate alerts. UEBA rules must be reviewed and tuned semi-annually. High-risk employees (e.g., those under HR investigation or in offboarding) may require enhanced monitoring with appropriate legal and HR review.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that behavioral baselines are established, anomaly detection alerts are configured and operational, and UEBA rules are reviewed semi-annually.

props.name: domain-description
props.value: Controls governing detection, response, forensics, and governance of security incidents and policy exceptions involving SaaS platforms.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Detect and automatically block or quarantine high-risk behaviors within SaaS platforms through policy-driven controls.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: At minimum, automated controls must cover: login from impossible travel, bulk data export exceeding defined thresholds, access from blocked IP ranges, and authentication from unmanaged devices. Policies must be reviewed quarterly. Automated blocks must generate SIEM alerts.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that automated threat policies are active, configured for the required risk scenarios, and that triggered events generate alerts routed to the security operations team.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Ensure all high-risk SaaS security alerts are triaged within SLA and escalated to the appropriate incident response team.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: SLA targets: Critical alerts — 15 minutes to acknowledge, 1 hour to triage; High alerts — 1 hour to acknowledge, 4 hours to triage. All alerts must route through the SIEM to the on-call SOC queue. Unacknowledged alerts must auto-escalate.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that alert routing from SaaS platforms to SIEM/SOC is functional, that SLA targets are defined, and that mean-time-to-acknowledge (MTTA) for critical alerts is within target.

controls.title: Incident Response Plan and Tabletop Testing

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Maintain a documented incident response plan covering SaaS platform security events and conduct tabletop exercises or simulations at least annually.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: The IR plan must address: SaaS-specific scenarios (account takeover, bulk data exfiltration, OAuth token compromise, vendor breach), escalation paths to application owners and legal, regulatory notification obligations by regulation and timeline, and evidence preservation procedures for SaaS-hosted data. Annual tabletop exercises must include at least one SaaS-specific scenario. Exercise results must be documented with identified gaps tracked to closure.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that a SaaS-inclusive IR plan exists, annual tabletop exercises have occurred, and gaps identified in exercises are tracked and remediated.

controls.title: Evidence Preservation and Cloud Forensics Readiness

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Ensure that SaaS platform audit logs and forensic artifacts can be preserved in a legally defensible manner to support incident investigations and e-discovery requests.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence preservation procedures must include: legal hold capability for SaaS-hosted data (email, files, records), chain-of-custody documentation for exported evidence, SIEM log preservation at original fidelity without modification, and SaaS vendor cooperation procedures for law enforcement requests. Log exports used as evidence must include cryptographic hash verification. Preservation procedures must be tested annually.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that legal hold procedures cover SaaS-hosted data, exported evidence includes chain-of-custody documentation, and forensic preservation procedures are tested annually.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Enforce time-bound, formally approved exceptions to security baseline controls, with documented compensating controls and periodic review.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: All exceptions to SSCF baseline controls must be: approved by the Security Team and the platform owner, documented with business justification and compensating controls, time-limited (maximum 12 months with mandatory renewal), and tracked in the risk register. Exceptions must not be used to defer indefinitely — unrenewed exceptions must revert to the baseline control.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Verify that all active exceptions have documented approvals, compensating controls, expiry dates within 12 months, and are tracked in the risk register.

resources.title: SSCF v0 Catalog (14-control baseline — backward compatibility reference)
